{{Short description|Psychological concept}}
In [[psychology]], '''grit''' is a positive, non-cognitive [[Trait theory|trait]] based on a person's [[wikt:Special:Search/perseverance|perseverance]] of effort combined with their passion for a particular long-term goal or end state (a powerful [[motivation]] to achieve an objective). This perseverance of effort helps people overcome obstacles or challenges to accomplishment and drives people to achieve.

Distinct but commonly associated concepts within the field of psychology include ''perseverance'', ''[[Hardiness (psychological)|hardiness]]'', ''[[Psychological resilience|resilience]]'', ''[[ambition (character trait)|ambition]]'', ''[[need for achievement]]'', ''[[conscientiousness]]'', and ''[[wikt:Special:Search/tenacity|tenacity]]''. These constructs can be conceptualized as [[Differential psychology|individual differences]] related to the accomplishment of work rather than as talent or ability. This distinction was brought into focus in 1907 when [[William James]] challenged psychology to further investigate how certain people can access richer trait reservoirs that enable them to accomplish more than the average person.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = James | first1 = W. | year = 1907 | title = The energies of men | journal = [[Science (journal)|Science]] | volume = 25 | issue = 635| pages = 321–332 | doi=10.1126/science.25.635.321| pmid = 17736950 | bibcode = 1907Sci....25..321J }}</ref> However, the construct of grit dates back at least to [[Francis Galton]],<ref name="Galton1892"/> and the ideals of persistence and tenacity have been understood as a virtue at least since [[Aristotle]].<ref>{{cite book|author=Aristotle|title=The Nicomachean Ethics|title-link=Nicomachean Ethics|at=VII}}</ref>

==Definition==
Grit was defined as "perseverance and passion for long-term goals" by psychologist [[Angela Duckworth]] and colleagues, who extensively studied grit as a personality trait.<ref name="Duckworth2007"/> They observed that people high in grit were able to maintain their determination and motivation over long periods despite experiences with failure and adversity.<ref name="Duckworth2007"/> They concluded that grit is a better predictor of success than intellectual talent ([[Intelligence quotient|IQ]]), based on their evaluation of educational attainment by adults; [[Grade point average|GPA]] among Ivy League undergraduates; dropout rate of cadets at [[United States Military Academy|West Point US Military Academy]]; and ranking in the [[National Spelling Bee]].<ref name="Duckworth2007">{{cite journal|last1=Duckworth|first1=A.L.|last2=Peterson|first2=C.|last3=Matthews|first3=M.D.|last4=Kelly|first4=D.R.|s2cid=11159170|date=June 2007|title=Grit: Perseverance and passion for long-term goals|journal=[[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]|volume=92|issue=6|pages=1087–1101|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.92.6.1087|pmid=17547490}}</ref>

Earlier studies of achievement often emphasized the notion that high-achieving people typically possess traits above and beyond that of normal ability.<ref name="Galton1892">{{cite book|last=Galton|first=Francis|author-link=Francis Galton|year=1892|title=Hereditary genius|location=New York|publisher=Appleton}}</ref><ref>{{multiref2
|1={{cite book|last=Cox|first=Catharine M.|year=1926|title=Genetic studies of genius|volume=2: The early mental traits of three hundred geniuses|editor-last=Terman|editor-first=Lewis M.|location=Stanford, Calif.|publisher=[[Stanford University Press]]}}
|2={{cite book|last1=Terman|first1=L.M.|last2=Oden|first2=M.H.|year=1947|title=The gifted child grows up: Twenty-five years' follow-up of a superior group|location=Stanford, Calif.|publisher=[[Stanford University Press]]}}
}}</ref> Duckworth ''et al.'' emphasized that grit is a better predictor of achievement than intellectual talent (IQ), because grit provides the stamina required to "stay the course" amid challenges and setbacks.<ref name="Duckworth2007" />

Marcus Crede and colleagues later observed that the contribution of grit to the prediction of success mostly stems from the perseverance of effort, and they questioned the inclusion of consistency of interest (passion) as one of the aspects of grit, as defined by Duckworth ''et al.''<ref name="Crede2016" />

==Positive psychology==
Grit ties in with [[positive psychology]] and in particular, with its promotion of perseverance: the ability to stick with and pursue a goal over a long period is an aspect of grit. This area of positive psychology considers perseverance as a positive indicator of long-term success.<ref name="Peterson2004">{{cite book|last1=Peterson|first1=C.|last2=Seligman|first2=M.E.|year=2004|title=Character strengths and virtues: A handbook and classification|location=Oxford|publisher=Oxford University Press}}</ref> One study found that individual differences in grit and its two component facets (perseverance of effort and consistency of interests over time) may derive in part from differences in what makes people happy.<ref name="VonCulin2014">{{cite journal |last1=Von Culin |first1=Katherine R. |last2=Tsukayama |first2=Eli |last3=Duckworth |first3=Angela L. |date=July 2014 |title=Unpacking grit: Motivational correlates of perseverance and passion for long-term goals |journal=[[The Journal of Positive Psychology]] |volume=9 |issue=4 |pages=306–312 |doi=10.1080/17439760.2014.898320|pmid=31404261 |pmc=6688745 }}</ref>

Grit is closely linked to resilience within the field of positive psychology. Individuals with higher levels of grit are able to bounce back from set backs more quickly due to a positive mindset. Grit highlights perseverance in working towards lifelong goals. These individuals tend to experience more success and demonstrate a more sustained effort over longer periods of time.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Vinothkumar |first1=M. |last2=Prasad |first2=Nayana |date=2016 |title=Moderating role of resilience in the relationship between grit and psychological well-being |url=http://www.indianjournals.com/ijor.aspx?target=ijor:ijpp3&volume=4&issue=2&article=002 |journal=International Journal of Psychology and Psychiatry |language=en |volume=4 |issue=2 |pages=10 |doi=10.5958/2320-6233.2016.00009.2 |issn=2320-6233|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==Intelligence==
One of the best predictors of future achievement has been [[intelligence]].<ref name="Gottfredson1997">{{cite journal | last1 = Gottfredson | first1 = L.S. | year = 1997 | title = Why ''g'' matters: The complexity of everyday life | url = https://www1.udel.edu/educ/gottfredson/reprints/1997whygmatters.pdf | journal = Intelligence | volume = 24 | pages = 79–132 | doi=10.1016/s0160-2896(97)90014-3| citeseerx = 10.1.1.535.4596 }}</ref> This relationship has been found in scholastic achievement as well as in job performance.<ref name="Neisser1996">{{cite journal | last1 = Neisser | first1 = U. | last2 = Boodoo | first2 = G. | last3 = Bouchard | first3 = T.J. | last4 = Boykin | first4 = A.W. | last5 = Brody | first5 = N. |display-authors=etal | year = 1996 | title = Intelligence: Knowns and unknowns | title-link=Intelligence: Knowns and Unknowns| journal = [[American Psychologist]] | volume = 51 | issue = 2| pages = 77–101 | doi=10.1037/0003-066x.51.2.77}}</ref> As such, one might expect that grit would be strongly correlated with intelligence. This prompted an early question of grit research: "Why do some individuals accomplish more than others of equal intelligence?".<ref name="Duckworth2007"/> Somewhat surprisingly, in four separate samples, grit was found to be either [[orthogonal]] to or slightly inversely [[correlated]] with intelligence.<ref name="Duckworth2009">{{cite journal | last1 = Duckworth | first1 = A.L. | last2 = Quinn | first2 = P.D. | year = 2009 | title = Development and validation of the Short Grit Scale (GRIT–S) | url = http://www.sas.upenn.edu/~duckwort/images/Duckworth%20and%20Quinn.GritS.pdf | journal = Journal of Personality Assessment | volume = 91 | issue = 2 | pages = 166–174 | doi = 10.1080/00223890802634290 | pmid = 19205937 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.616.5639 | s2cid = 15232924 | access-date = 2012-04-26 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160609211216/http://www.sas.upenn.edu/~duckwort/images/Duckworth%20and%20Quinn.GritS.pdf | archive-date = 2016-06-09 | url-status = dead }}</ref> This means that grit, unlike many traditional measures of performance, is not tied to intelligence. The researchers suggested that this helps explain why some very intelligent people do not consistently perform well over long periods.

==Personality measure==
The grit measure has been compared to the [[Big Five personality traits]], which are a group of broad personality dimensions consisting of [[openness to experience]], [[conscientiousness]], [[extraversion]], [[agreeableness]], and [[neuroticism]].<ref name="Goldberg1990">{{cite journal | last1 = Goldberg | first1 = L.R. | s2cid = 9034636 | year = 1990 | title = An alternative 'description of personality': The big-five factor structure | journal = [[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]] | volume = 59 | issue = 6| pages = 1216–1229 | doi=10.1037/0022-3514.59.6.1216 | pmid=2283588}}</ref> In one study, the Short Grit Scale (Grit–S) and 12-item self-report measure of grit (Grit–O) measuring grit were strongly correlated with conscientiousness (''r''&nbsp;=&nbsp;0.77, ''p''&nbsp;<&nbsp;0.001 and ''r''&nbsp;=&nbsp;0.73, ''p''&nbsp;<&nbsp;0.001). A large study of twins measuring both conscientiousness and grit found that they had a [[genetic correlation]] of 0.86.<ref name="Rimfeld2016" /> A subsequent meta-analysis found that grit was functionally a measure of conscientiousness.<ref name="Crede2016">{{Cite journal|title = Much ado about grit: A meta-analytic synthesis of the grit literature|journal = [[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]|volume = 113|issue = 3|pages = 492–511|doi = 10.1037/pspp0000102|first1 = Marcus|last1 = Crede|first2 = Michael|last2 = Tynan|first3 = Peter|last3 = Harms|pmid=27845531|year = 2017|s2cid = 24361685}}</ref> Despite these high correlations with conscientiousness, {{clarify|text=it is suggested that|reason=does the evidence show this? if so, say so; if not, say who is making this suggestion and on what basis|date=July 2023}} grit is more strongly associated with longer term and multi-year goals such as education.<ref name="Duckworth2007"/> Large studies suggest only weak links of grit to these long-term goals,<ref name="Rimfeld2016" /> much smaller than {{clarify|text=links to|reason=ambiguous: links of grit to IQ/conscientiousness, or links of long-term-goals to IQ/conscientiousness?|date=July 2023}} IQ and to conscientiousness. Grit is also similar to [[need for achievement]], but is suggested{{by whom|date=July 2023}} to relate to extremely long-term goals pursued without positive feedback, while need for achievement is suggested to lack this long-term component.<ref name="Duckworth2007" />

Grit may be domain-specific rather than a domain-general trait.<ref name="Cormier2019">{{cite journal | last1 = Cormier | first1 = D.L. | last2 = Dunn | first2 = J.G. | last3 = Dunn | first3 = J.C. | year = 2019 | title = Examining the domain specificity of grit | journal = [[Personality and Individual Differences]] | volume = 139 | pages = 349–354 | doi = 10.1016/j.paid.2018.11.026| s2cid = 149990034 }}</ref> One prolific area of research looked at its role in second language learning or L2{{definition needed|L2 was originally short hand for “second language”. It assumed that learners grew up with one native language and then add a second language. This is, however, not always the case. So, now, it is used to refer to the language that someone is learning in addition to their native language(s). Such as, she learned Spanish and English from her parents but is now adding Arabic as an L2.|date=July 2023}} grit.<ref>{{multiref2
|1={{cite journal | last1 = Alamer | first1 = A. | year = 2021 | title = Grit and language learning: Construct validation of L2-Grit scale (L2 meaning second language) and its relation to later vocabulary knowledge | journal = [[Educational Psychology]] | volume = 41 | issue = 5 | pages = 544–562 | doi=10.1080/01443410.2020.1867076| s2cid = 234306409 }}
|2={{cite journal | last1 = Teimouri | first1 = Y. | last2 = Plonsky | first2 = L. | last3 = Tabandeh | first3 = F. | year = 2022 | title = L2 grit: Passion and perseverance for second-language learning | journal = [[Language Teaching Research]] | volume = 26 | issue = 5 | pages = 893–918 | doi=10.1177/1362168820921895| s2cid = 219750829 | doi-access = free}}
}}</ref> Grit is not only domain-specific, but also context-dependent, with its predictors differing in face-to-face vs. online learning contexts.<ref name="Paradowski2023">{{cite journal | last1 = Paradowski | first1 = M.B. | last2 = Jelińska | first2 = M. | year = 2023 | title = The predictors of L2 grit and their complex interactions in online foreign language learning: Motivation, self-directed learning, autonomy, curiosity, and language mindsets | journal = [[Computer Assisted Language Learning]] | volume = 37 | issue = 8 | pages = 1–38 | doi=10.1080/09588221.2023.2192762| s2cid = 258897137 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

==Related psychological constructs==
Traditional constructs in this area include [[:wikt:perseverance|perseverance]], [[Hardiness (psychological)|hardiness]], [[psychological resilience|resilience]], [[:wikt:ambition|ambition]], [[self-control]], and [[need for achievement]]. Grit has been argued to be distinguishable from each of these in the following ways.

;perseverance: Perseverance is the steadfast pursuit of a task, mission, or journey in spite of obstacles, discouragement, or distraction. In contrast, grit is argued to be a trait of perseverance. Grit enables a person to persevere in accomplishing a goal despite obstacles over an extended period.<ref name="Duckworth2007"/> When compared with the construct of persistence, grit adds the component of passion for the goal.<ref name="Hancock2008">{{cite book|last1=Hancock|first1=P.A.|last2=Szalma|first2=J.L.|year=2008|chapter=Stress and performance|editor-last1=Hancock|editor-first1=P.A.|editor-last2=Szalma|editor-first2=J.L.|title=Performance under stress|location=Cornwall|publisher=MPG Books}}{{page needed|date=August 2019}}</ref> This goal-passion contributes to the ability of the person to sustain effort over the long term.

;hardiness: Salvatore Maddi defined hardiness as a combination of attitudes that provide the courage and motivation to do the hard, strategic work of turning stressful circumstances from potential disasters into growth opportunities.<ref name="Maddi2006">{{cite journal | last1 = Maddi | first1 = Salvatore R. | s2cid = 32269322 | year = 2006 | title = Hardiness: The courage to grow from stresses | journal = [[The Journal of Positive Psychology]] | volume = 1 | issue = 3| pages = 160–168 | doi=10.1080/17439760600619609}}</ref> While grit is primarily a measure of a person's ability to persist in obtaining a specific goal over an extended time period,<ref name="Duckworth2007"/> hardiness refers to a person's ability to persist through difficult circumstances and does not address the person's long term persistence toward a specific goal.<ref name="Duckworth2007"/> Maddi developed a theoretical model of hardiness as a tool for developing resilience.<ref name="Maddi2006"/>

;resilience: Resilience is a process in which a person overcomes significant adversity, usually in the form of a life-changing event or difficult personal circumstances. Resilience is an adaptive response to a challenging situation.<ref name="LDZ1993">{{cite journal | last1 = Luthar | first1 = S. | last2 = Doernberger | first2 = C.H. | last3 = Zigler | first3 = E. | year = 1993 | title = Resilience is not a unidimensional construct: Insights from a prospective study on inner-city adolescents | journal = [[Development and Psychopathology]] | volume = 5 | issue = 4| pages = 703–717 | doi=10.1017/s0954579400006246| pmid = 25722542 | pmc = 4339070 }}</ref> Grit involves maintaining goal-focused effort for extended periods of time, often while facing adversity, but it does not require a critical incident. Importantly, grit is conceptualized as a trait while resilience is a process. Finally, resilience has been almost exclusively studied in children who are born into "at-risk" situations.<ref name="LDZ1993"/> Although resilience researchers recognize that adults likely demonstrate resilience in a similar manner to children, {{as of|1996|alt=as of 1996}} the resilience process has not been studied in a mature population.<ref name="Schulz1996">{{cite journal | last1 = Schulz | first1 = R. | last2 = Heckhausen | first2 = J. |author-link2=Jutta Heckhausen| year = 1996 | title = A life-span model of successful aging | journal = [[American Psychologist]] | volume = 51 | issue = 7| pages = 702–714 | doi=10.1037/0003-066x.51.7.702 | pmid=8694390| citeseerx = 10.1.1.559.9580 }}</ref>

;ambition: Ambition is the desire for attainment, power, or superiority. In contrast to ambition, grit is not associated with seeking fame or external recognition for achievements. Ambition is often associated with a desire for fame.<ref name="Maltby2008">{{cite journal | last1 = Maltby | first1 = J. | last2 = Day | first2 = L. | last3 = Giles | first3 = D. | last4 = Gillett | first4 = R. | last5 = Quick | first5 = M. | last6 = Langcaster-James | first6 = H. | last7 = Linley | first7 = P.A. | s2cid = 9905852 | year = 2008 | title = Implicit theories of a desire for fame | journal = [[British Journal of Psychology]] | volume = 99 | issue = Part 2| pages = 279–292 | doi=10.1348/000712607x226935 |pmid=17637166| url = http://shura.shu.ac.uk/6051/4/Day_desire_for_fame.pdf }}</ref> Unlike ambitious people, gritty people do not seek to distinguish themselves from others, but to achieve personal goals. Grit has also been linked to a decrease in burnout, an increase in performance, and even minimized depression.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Schimschal |first=Sarah |date=September 11, 2020 |title=Grit: A Concept Analysis |url=https://doi.org/10.1080/01612840.2020.1814913 |journal=Journal of Multidisciplinary Healthcare |volume=42 |issue=5 |pages=495–505 |doi=10.1080/01612840.2020.1814913 |pmid=32915678 |via=Taylor and Francis Online|url-access=subscription }}</ref> These positive side effects of grit can be attributed to psychological devices that limit harmful effects by replacing them with cognitive process that promote productivity and achievement.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Masicampo |first1=E. J. |last2=Baumeister |first2=Roy F. |date=2011 |title=Consider it done! Plan making can eliminate the cognitive effects of unfulfilled goals. |url=http://doi.apa.org/getdoi.cfm?doi=10.1037/a0024192 |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |language=en |volume=101 |issue=4 |pages=667–683 |doi=10.1037/a0024192 |pmid=21688924 |issn=1939-1315|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

;self-control: An aspect of [[inhibitory control]], self-control is the ability to control one's emotions and behavior in the face of temptations and impulses. Duckworth and [[James Gross]] used a hierarchical-goal perspective on self-control and grit to show that while both of these constructs entail aligning actions with intentions, they operate in different ways and over different timescales and are distinct psychological mechanisms that are key determinants of success.<ref name="DuckworthGross2014">{{cite journal | last1 = Duckworth | first1 = Angela L. | last2 = Gross | first2 = James J. | year = 2014 | title = Self-control and grit: Related but separable determinants of success | journal = [[Current Directions in Psychological Science]] | volume = 23 | issue = 5| pages = 319–325 | doi=10.1177/0963721414541462 |pmid=26855479 |pmc=4737958}}</ref>

;need for achievement: [[David McClelland]] described need for achievement as a drive to complete manageable goals that enable the person to receive immediate [[feedback]].<ref name="McClelland1961">{{cite book|last=McClelland|first=D.C.|year=1961|title=The achieving society|location=Oxford, England|publisher=Van Nostrand}}{{page needed|date=August 2019}}</ref> In contrast to need for achievement, gritty people consciously set long-term goals that are difficult to attain and do not waver from these difficult goals, regardless of the presence of feedback. Need for achievement has been studied for almost 50 years and has been found to positively correlate to [[self-efficacy]] and learning [[goal orientation]].<ref>{{multiref2
|1={{cite journal | last1 = Phillips | first1 = J.M. | last2 = Gully | first2 = S.M. | year = 1997 | title = Role of goal orientation, ability, need for achievement, and locus of control in the self-efficacy and goal-setting process | journal = [[Journal of Applied Psychology]] | volume = 82 | issue = 5| pages = 792–802 | doi=10.1037/0021-9010.82.5.792}}
|2={{cite journal |last1=Heintz |first1=Paul |last2=Steele-Johnson |first2=Debra |date=January 2004 |title=Clarifying the conceptual definitions of goal orientation dimensions: Competence, control, and evaluation |journal=Organizational Analysis |volume=12 |issue=1 |pages=5–19 |doi=10.1108/eb028983}}
}}</ref> These links have not yet{{Update inline|date=July 2023}} been tested in the grit literature.

==Scientific findings and controversy==
The primary scientific findings on grit come from Duckworth and colleagues' 2007 examination of grit as an individual difference trait capable of predicting long-term success.<ref name="Duckworth2007"/> A subsequent meta-analysis of the structure and correlates of grit questioned Duckworth's construct of grit (that included both the perseverance of effort facet and the consistency of interest facet), concluding that the primary utility of the grit construct may stem from the perseverance of effort.<ref name="Crede2016"/>

Duckworth initially proposed that people with a drive to tirelessly work through challenges, [[failure]]s, and adversity to achieve set goals are better positioned to reach higher achievements than people who lack similar stamina.<ref name="Duckworth2007"/> In a series of six studies Duckworth ''et al.'' proposed, developed, and tested a two-factor grit scale. In addition to validating their grit scale, the authors also found
* that grit provided incremental {{clarify|text=predictive|reason=predicting what? education & age?|date=July 2023}} validity for education and age above and beyond the [[Big Five personality traits]] 
* that higher levels of grit were more highly associated with cumulative grade point average ([[GPA]]) in an [[Ivy league]] sample when compared to those with lower grit levels (''r''&nbsp;=&nbsp;0.25, ''p''&nbsp;<&nbsp;0.01)
* that grit predicted [[Employee retention|retention]] after their first summer in two classes of cadets at the [[United States Military Academy]]
* that participants in a [[National Spelling Bee]] with higher grit scores typically work harder and longer than less gritty peers, ultimately resulting in better performance.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Duckworth |first1=Angela Lee |last2=Kirby |first2=Teri A. |last3=Tsukayama |first3=Eli |last4=Berstein |first4=Heather |last5=Ericsson |first5=K. Anders |title=Deliberate Practice Spells Success: Why Grittier Competitors Triumph at the National Spelling Bee |journal=Social Psychological and Personality Science |date=March 2011 |volume=2 |issue=2 |pages=174–181 |doi=10.1177/1948550610385872|s2cid=10500100 }}</ref>
This series of studies provides [[empirical evidence]] that an individual difference conceptualized as grit can account for significant [[variance]] in performance across a variety of settings.

Although Duckworth argued that grit predicted academic performance better than the Big Five personality traits, that claim was later called into question by Kaili Rimfeld and colleagues, who argued that Big Five personality factors have equal predictive ability,<ref name="Rimfeld2016" /> and by Crede, who concluded that grit is very strongly correlated with conscientiousness, and that after controlling for conscientiousness, only one component of grit (perseverance of effort) explains variance in academic performance.<ref name="Crede2016" />

The largest study of grit in the United Kingdom, based on academic achievement of 2,321 twin pairs (U.K.-representative sample and genetically sensitive design), compared the predictive ability of grit as a trait (measured by the Grit-S) to the predictions based on Big Five personality traits. It found that while personality is a significant predictor of academic achievement, grit as a separate construct added little to the prediction of academic achievement derived from Big Five personality factors, such as conscientiousness.<ref name="Rimfeld2016">{{multiref2
|1={{Cite journal|title = True grit and genetics: Predicting academic achievement from personality|year=2016|journal = [[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]|doi = 10.1037/pspp0000089|first1 = Kaili|last1 = Rimfeld|first2 = Yulia|last2 = Kovas|first3 = Philip S.|last3 = Dale|first4 = Robert|last4 = Plomin|pmid=26867111|pmc=4981570|volume=111|issue=5|pages=780–789}}
|2={{cite web |last=Rimfeld |first=Kaili |date=12 February 2016 |title=Why a bit of grit won't get children higher grades |url=https://theconversation.com/why-a-bit-of-grit-wont-get-children-higher-grades-54504 |website=theconversation.com |publisher=[[The Conversation (website)|The Conversation]] |access-date=2018-04-08}}
}}</ref>

A meta-analytic synthesis of empirical research on grit summarized data from 88 independent samples and over 66,000 people, and found that grit is only moderately correlated with performance, and that only one component of grit (perseverance of effort) explains variance in academic performance. The study consequently suggested to separate consistency of interest (passion) from perseverance (effort), since the contribution of grit to the prediction of success mostly stems from the perseverance facet.<ref name="Crede2016" />

Psychologist [[K. Anders Ericsson]], in his book ''[[Peak: Secrets from the New Science of Expertise]]'', criticized the tendency to credit persistent practice to traits such as grit or willpower. He wrote: "It may seem natural to assume that these people who maintain intense practice schedules for years have some rare gift of willpower or 'grit' or 'stick-to-itiveness' that the rest of us just lack, but that would be a mistake for two very compelling reasons."<ref name="Ericsson">{{cite book |last1=Ericsson |first1=K. Anders |author-link1=K. Anders Ericsson |last2=Pool |first2=Robert |year=2016 |title=Peak: secrets from the new science of expertise |location=Boston |publisher=[[Houghton Mifflin Harcourt]] |pages=[https://archive.org/details/peaksecretsfromn0000eric/page/167 167–168] |isbn=9780544456235 |oclc=913923802 |url=https://archive.org/details/peaksecretsfromn0000eric/page/167 }}</ref> The first reason is that motivation is a situation-specific attribute: People generally find it easier to practice in some areas{{ambiguous|date=July 2023}} than in others.<ref name="Ericsson"/> The second reason is that grit and willpower are traits that are assigned to someone after the fact—for example: John practiced persistently for years, so he must have incredible grit.<ref name="Ericsson"/> But, Ericsson explained: "This sort of circular thinking—'The fact that I couldn't keep practicing indicates that I don't have enough willpower, which explains why I couldn't keep practicing'—is worse than useless; it is damaging in that it can convince people that they might as well not even try."<ref name="Ericsson"/> Instead of attributing success to grit or willpower, Ericsson recommended analyzing the various factors that shape a person's motivation in a given situation.

{{As of|2014|since=y}}, grit has been the subject of critical commentary and debate in ''[[Education Week]]'', with contributors discussing the strengths and weaknesses of how the idea of grit has been used by educators.<ref name="Edweek"/> Some contributors called "the grit narrative" a kind of [[victim blaming]] when educators who emphasize grit downplay the obstacles that some students face such as conditions of poverty, racism, and ineffective teaching.<ref name="Edweek">Among the relevant articles and blog posts in ''[[Education Week]]'' are:
* {{cite news|url=https://blogs.edweek.org/edweek/independent_schools/2014/03/whats_dangerous_about_the_grit_narrative_and_how_to_fix_it.html|title=What's dangerous about the grit narrative, and how to fix it|last=Gow|first=Peter|date=3 March 2014|newspaper=[[Education Week]]|access-date=2018-01-18|quote=There's been a great deal of buzz lately on the topic of grit. As educators we're all for persistence, resilience, stick-to-it-ive-ness—the stuff of grit, right? But it turns out that there's grit, and then there's the way some people are talking about grit.}}
* {{cite news|url=https://blogs.edweek.org/teachers/living-in-dialogue/2014/03/lauren_anderson_grit.html|title=Grit, Galton, and eugenics|last=Anderson|first=Lauren|date=21 March 2014|newspaper=[[Education Week]]|access-date=2018-01-18|quote=I was unsurprised to find that the areas of focus were 'grit' and 'self control'—now popular concepts celebrated in mass-market texts like Paul Tough's ''How Children Succeed'' and Jay Mathews's ''Work Hard, Play Nice'', and an appealing policy target for those who believe that if we could just cultivate the 'right' qualities among the 'low-achieving' then they would be able to transcend conditions of poverty and other obstacles in their way.}}
* {{cite journal|last=Kohn|first=Alfie|author-link=Alfie Kohn|date=9 September 2014|title=The deferred-gratification myth|url=https://www.edweek.org/ew/articles/2014/09/09/03kohn.h34.html|journal=[[Education Week]]|volume=34|issue=3|pages=32–26|quote=The more effort we devote to getting students to pay 'attention to a teacher rather than daydreaming' and persist 'on long-term assignments despite boredom and frustration' (in the words of 'grit' proponent Angela Duckworth), the less likely we are to ask whether those assignments are actually worth doing, or to rethink an arrangement where teachers mostly talk and students mostly listen.}}
* {{cite journal|last=Herold|first=Benjamin|date=4 February 2015|title=Some educators are calling the concept of 'grit' racist|url=https://www.edweek.org/ew/articles/2015/02/04/blogs-of-the-week-1.html|journal=[[Education Week]]|volume=34|issue=20|pages=8|quote=Increasingly, though, critics are offering a different take, arguing that grit is a racist construct and has harmed low-income students by crowding out a focus on providing children with the supports they deserve and the more-flexible educational approach enjoyed by many of their more affluent counterparts.}}
* {{cite journal|last=Hoerr|first=Thomas R.|date=18 February 2015|title='Grit' helps everyone gain real-world success|url=https://www.edweek.org/ew/articles/2015/02/18/grit-helps-everyone-gain-real-world-success.html|journal=[[Education Week]]|volume=34|issue=21|pages=26|quote=Believe it or not, some folks think fostering grit is lowering expectations for students or failing to appreciate the obstacles they face. While there are some who may misunderstand or misapply the teaching of grit, it's wrong to assume that these misapplications are representative of grit's true purpose: to help all students learn to succeed in the real world.}}
* {{cite news|url=https://blogs.edweek.org/edweek/learning_deeply/2015/04/the_problem_with_grit.html|title=The problem with grit|last=Mehta|first=Jal|date=27 April 2015|newspaper=[[Education Week]]|access-date=2018-01-18|quote=As with any concept that gains popularity, there have been detractors. The most prominent critique is that an emphasis on grit is a way of 'blaming the victim'—rather than take up larger questions of social, economic, and racial justice, if only the most disadvantaged kids were a little 'grittier' they could make it in life.}}
* {{cite journal|last=Blad|first=Evie|date=20 May 2015|title=Accountability measures for traits like 'grit' questioned|url=https://www.edweek.org/ew/articles/2015/05/20/accountability-measures-for-traits-like-grit-questioned.html|journal=[[Education Week]]|volume=34|issue=31|pages=6|quote=Measurements of so-called noncognitive student traits like self-control, 'grit,' and gratitude should not be used for school accountability or teacher-evaluation purposes, two pioneers in the field warned last week.}}
* {{cite news|url=https://blogs.edweek.org/teachers/classroom_qa_with_larry_ferlazzo/2015/10/response_is_grit_an_asset_or_an_excuse.html|title=Response: 'It's time to change the conversation about grit'|last=Ferlazzo|first=Larry|date=5 October 2015|newspaper=[[Education Week]]|access-date=2018-01-18|quote=At the same time, however, I am wary of pushing the 'grit narrative' too far, as some have done already by proclaiming what I call The Let Them Eat Character strategy. It is in the self-interest of many in our society to use the 'all it takes is hard work' mantra as a public excuse for perpetuating political and economic policies that thwart the dreams of many because of their race or economic class.}}
* {{cite news|url=https://blogs.edweek.org/edweek/walt_gardners_reality_check/2016/04/how_important_is_grit_for_success.html|title=How important is grit for success?|last=Gardner|first=Walt|date=22 April 2016|newspaper=[[Education Week]]|access-date=2018-01-18|quote=There's no doubt that the concept helps explain why some students achieve in the face of daunting odds ('Don't Grade Schools on Grit,' ''The New York Times'', Mar. 26). But I think it is a gross oversimplification.}}
* {{cite news|url=https://blogs.edweek.org/edweek/K-12_Contrarian/2016/06/bad_grit.html|title=When having grit is a bad thing|last=Powell|first=Dave|date=3 June 2016|newspaper=[[Education Week]]|access-date=2018-01-18|quote=Grit is one of those ideas that, as we say, has legs. That's clear enough. But is it good for us?}}
* {{cite journal|last=Yeh|first=Christine|date=14 April 2017|title=Forget grit. Focus on inequality. Why is grit at the center of the national ed. debate?|url=https://www.edweek.org/ew/articles/2017/04/14/forget-grit-focus-on-inequality.html|journal=[[Education Week]]|volume=36|issue=28|quote=The notion of grit has certainly spurred important discussions about the nonacademic experiences and skills we want our students to have, but it has often obscured the very conditions that created educational inequities in the first place.}}</ref>

The idea that grit can be enhanced was also criticized.{{explain|reason=enhanced how? what's the gist of the criticism?|date=July 2023}}<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Moreau|first1=David|last2=Macnamara|first2=B|last3=Hambrick|first3=D|date=2019-02-01|title=Overstating the role of environmental factors in success: a cautionary note|journal=[[Current Directions in Psychological Science]]|language=en|volume=28|issue=1|pages=28–33|doi=10.1177/0963721418797300|s2cid=149536001|url=http://psyarxiv.com/sv9pz/|doi-access=free}}</ref>

A meta-analysis found that {{clarify|text=overall grit/2 facets|reason=define this|date=July 2023}} is {{clarify|text=cross-culturally related|reason=this too|date=July 2023}} to academic achievement.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Lam|first1=K.K.L|last2=Zhou|first2=M|year=2022|title=Grit and academic achievement: A comparative cross-cultural meta-analysis.|journal=[[Journal of Educational Psychology]]|language=en|volume=114|issue=3|pages=597–621|doi=10.1037/edu0000699|s2cid=239510690 }}</ref> However, the level of persistency and grit may vary among people of different cultures.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Pasha-Zaidi |first1=Nausheen |last2=Afari |first2=Ernest |last3=Sevi |first3=Barış |last4=Urganci |first4=Betul |last5=Durham |first5=Justin |title=Responsibility of learning: a cross‑cultural examination of the relationship of grit, motivational belief and self‑regulation among college students in the US, UAE and Turkey |journal=Learning Environments Research |year=2019 |volume=22 |pages=83–100 |doi=10.1007/s10984-018-9268-y |s2cid=254753766 |url=https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10984-018-9268-y|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

A study completed by Müge Akbağ and Durmuş Ümmet<ref>{{Cite web |last=Akbağ |first=Müge |date=October 2017 |title=Predictive Role of Grit and Basic Psychological Needs Satisfaction on Subjective Well-Being for Young Adults |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/320273643 |access-date=November 6, 2023}}</ref> examined the role of gender in terms of grit as well as the satisfaction of psychological needs and subjective well-being. Akbağ and Ümmet found that female psychological need satisfaction was on average higher than those of males. The predictive role of grit was found to be linked to that of psychological need satisfaction in that when there was a higher level of satisfaction there was an increased level of personal grit as well. With this being said, there may be levels of variance depending on social and cultural situations as well.
